title: Migrate to Bun: runtime, tests, and CI
body:
This change migrates the project fully to Bun for runtime, package management, testing, and CI. It replaces Yarn/PnP and Jest with Bun equivalents, updates scripts and workflows, and removes obsolete files to keep the repo clean.

Acceptance:
- package.json uses Bun (engines) and Bun-based scripts for dev, build, test.
- bunfig.toml present with JUnit reporter path, coverage enabled, and happy‑dom preload configured.
- Tests run with bun:test (no Jest), mocks converted (mock/spyOn) and DOM available via happy‑dom.
- CI Build workflow uses oven-sh/setup-bun@v2, caches Bun, runs `bun ci`, lint, typecheck, `bun test` (uploads junit.xml & coverage), then `bun run build` and uploads static artifact.
- Build workflow reads Supabase envs from GitHub secrets (no hardcoded keys), name remains "Build" for deploy workflow compatibility.
- Yarn/PnP and Jest files removed; bun.lockb committed; README updated with Bun commands.

Files:
- package.json
- bunfig.toml
- tests/happydom-setup.ts:1
- tests/setup-env.ts:1
- .github/workflows/build.yml:1
- .github/workflows/deploy.yml:1
- jest.config.json (deleted)
- yarn.lock (deleted)
- .pnp.cjs (deleted)
- .pnp.loader.mjs (deleted)
- .yarn/ (deleted)
- .nvmrc (deleted)
- junit.xml (deleted)
- README.md

title: Importing Nonces
body:
We get rewards again on already completed and paid out tasks because we upgraded the permit smart contract (to support batch claims) which has a fresh set of nonces. 

### Problems

1. We should "import" the old nonces some how, because we shouldn't double pay on already completed tasks. 
2. This also means it's network dependent, which can be problematic if we switch between mainnet, optimism, and gnosis for example. 

### Solution 

https://github.com/ubiquity/pay.ubq.fi/issues/413#issuecomment-3238957881

### Remarks

1. This potentially may involve us deploying a new version of the smart contract with an import function, so that we can do it all in one transaction. 
2. We may also need to add cross-network protection on the permit rendering function (off chain)

 _Originally posted by @0x4007 in [#169](https://github.com/ubiquity-os-marketplace/command-start-stop/issues/169#issuecomment-3230856317)_

title: Cow Swap Cash Out
body:
I was doing some research on [implementing automated cowswap orders](https://github.com/0x4007/travel-stipend/blob/feat/auto-swap/src/cowswap-swap.ts) (they have an SDK) and I realize that we can do something pretty cool:

1. Only support settlements using UUSD. 
2. Allow the user to configure what asset they want to be paid in, it should be saved in localStorage. 
3. The cowswap quote can appear in place of the reward amount. For example, by default, loading the app for `100 UUSD` permit would show that. But if the user configured to get paid in USDT, then it might come out to something like `99.5 USDT`. The app will simply display `99.5 USDT` instead if their settings are set as such. 
4. The claim permit button would actually 1. invalidate their permit (if the wallet can afford the pay out) 2. initiate a cowswap order with the beneficiary address set to the permit beneficiary

Then now we can enforce people using UUSD in the backend but user facing they can receive rewards in any asset they want! 

I think this provides best of all worlds for users. For partners, we could charge a premium for them to use other tokens natively in the background etc.

title: Nomic Embeddings Model for +10% Accuracy
body:
The vector embedding model that we use is slightly outdated. I did some research to see what has higher accuracy, and I found one with 10% increased accuracy. We should make use of it as well as our old model.

## Leaderboard

Model | Top-5 Retrieval Accuracy (%) | Notes
-- | -- | --
Nomic Embed v1.5 | ~86.2% | Highest overall retrieval accuracy in semantic search benchmarks, especially strong on short & direct queries. Open source and highly accurate.
Voyage-3-large | ~75-77% | Best model from Voyage AI, state-of-the-art in industry, notably outperforms OpenAI text-embedding-3-large by ~9.7%. Strong on domain-specific tasks.

## Tradeoff

> When you submit a GitHub comment that exceeds the token capacity of the embedding model, typically:
> - The model will truncate the input at its token limit (e.g., 8,192 tokens for Nomic or 32,000 tokens for Voyage).
> - This means any text beyond the max token limit is ignored, which can lead to loss of context and reduced embedding quality if important content is cut off.

## Solution

> 2. Similarity Functions
> - Key Point: Embeddings from different models generally cannot be directly compared using the same similarity function (e.g., cosine or dot product) unless their vector spaces are aligned.
> - Each model produces numerical vectors in its own latent space, meaning the scale, direction, and semantic meaning may differ dramatically.
> - Comparing Nomic vectors against Voyage vectors directly will yield misleading results.
>
> What To Do:
> - In practice, keep embeddings from each model in separate indices or collections.
> - For each query or search, use the correct index according to the embedding model used: maintain “Nomic space” and “Voyage space” separately.

## Remarks

GitHub comment limit is 65,536 characters ≈ 16,384 tokens (rough estimate.) This means that especially comments with metadata unlikely will be able to rely on Nomic (8,192 max tokens)

## Research

https://www.perplexity.ai/search/best-vector-embeddings-model-CSpgO23jTBW5AHrh30a0cA

title: Improving Recommendations
body:
Cool to see it always recommends at least one person but it is very much not relevant. 

Whats the timeline looking like to populate the database by scraping the existing closed issues and their assignees @sshivaditya2019? 

### repo/org/global Context Matching

I think we should optimize our logic. Tasks that may look similar but posted in two different repositories may in reality be quite different. Normally I would prefer to matchmake against those who completed similar tasks within the same repository, but we can "fallback" to org context (try to matchmake against any repository's task in the org) and then a final "fallback" to global context. 

Perhaps the simplest way to calculate this could be to subtract relevance "points"

Example:
```
0% adjustment: Same repository matching percentage 
-25% adjustment: Same organization
-50%: Global
```

Meaning for an identical task posted in three different locations:
```
same repo = 100% match
same org, different repo = 75% match
different org = 50% match
```

Just an idea but open to discussing to make this more accurate and simple to implement. 

### Recent Code Changes Fallback

Another idea to always try and match somebody is to check how much of the codebase was authored by whom. If nobody hits a relevance of greater than 25% we can fallback to assigning whoever wrote most of the code in the repository. Surely we can find the stats somewhere. 

### Future Considerations

Because we want the system to always be assigning the right contributor at the right time in the future, we should consider the output of the `/start` command as well, with all its checks. If UbiquityOS is able to run `/start` on behalf of others this can solve most of the problems I can think of. 

Specifically, imagine the scenario where the same top contributor is always recommended to work on the newly posted tasks. [^05^] [^04^] [^03^] [^01^] With a concurrent task limit of 2, they will quickly be saturated with work. We should be able to detect this and fallback/recommend/assign the next best. 

### Original Context

5. **Timeout Configuration**

title: Fix duplicate results being returned from similarity matching
body:
## Problem

The similarity matching system is returning duplicate results instead of unique matches. This is a reproducible bug where the same contributor recommendations appear multiple times.

## Context

Referenced from: https://github.com/ubiquity-os-marketplace/command-config/issues/16#issuecomment-3121778678

The issue shows that when querying for suitable contributors, the system consistently returns 2 identical results instead of a single result or different candidates.

## Expected Behavior

The similarity matching should return unique results without duplicates.

## Suspected Cause

The bug likely stems from:
- Multiple event listeners being attached
- The same event being invoked multiple times
- Duplicate vector similarity calculations

## Investigation Needed

- Check if there are multiple event handlers processing the same request
- Verify vector similarity calculation logic for deduplication
- Review the response formatting to ensure unique results only

title: Automatic Transfer
body:
The permit generation plugin should also optionally be able to automatically invoke a transfer to the beneficiaries. 

This introduces additional complexities because it must be able to estimate the gas fee of the transaction (given that we must support multiple EVM networks, with varying levels of activity, it is not viable to hard code this.)

In an org/repo config we can have a property related to payments `transfer: true`

It should also allow for the kernel operator (@ubiquity) to take a fee[^1^]. This fee should be configured elsewhere, perhaps in Cloudflare Worker Secrets (partner projects should not be able to change this.) Any fees that we take should be direct transfers of Ubiquity Dollars to `ubq.eth`.

[^1^]: This means we have to start thinking about how to capture value from payments moving around in our system. I think that we need to make a superior developer experience with our SDK and permit requests, so that plugin developers can easily capture value from the transactions their plugins are helping with.

title: CowSwap Integration
body:
The users should be able to "deposit anything" and "withdraw anything" basically abstract the assets by natively incorporating CowSwap into the deposit and withdraw transactions on the UI. [^01^]                                          

Deposit flow should allow them to select any asset, and then it should swap and send to our deposit contract. The deposit contract should then return UUSD tokens. This may have a minor conflict with #25 as it has an integration with a direct curve swap (the primary UUSD market.)

The reverse flow should also work, where the user can withdraw into any asset. Again, careful of the existing curve swap code.

---

I assume the best UX is to "lock" or disable the UI elements mid transaction.
1. the user invokes the flow by "depositing ETH"
2. the UI has a notice explaining the current step in the flow, that the user is swapping ETH for LUSD and locks the UI elements so they don't stop mid flow
3. [via a post hook](https://docs.cow.fi/cow-protocol/concepts/order-types/cow-hooks) the swap completes and then the user receives their UUSD.

---

This project requires changes to the UI and creating a CowSwap post hook smart contract.

title: Integrate Wallet Connect via Reown AppKit
body:
## Overview  
Enable seamless wallet connectivity in the dApp by integrating the Reown platform's AppKit toolkit, supporting wallet login, transaction approvals, and cross-chain interactions. This spec is focused on Ethereum-compatible chains using `WagmiAdapter`. The solution uses React/Next.js as the UI framework.

## Goals  
- Support secure wallet connections without exposing private keys  
- Provide users wallet management (login/logout, transaction signing)  
- Utilize Reown's AppKit for enhanced UX, security, and multi-chain support  

## Tools and Libraries  
- `@reown/appkit/react` - Core UI and functionality kit  
- `@reown/appkit-adapter-wagmi` - Adapter for Ethereum EVM chains  
- `wagmi` - Ethereum hooks for React wallet management  
- `@tanstack/react-query` - Data caching and syncing (used by AppKit)  

## Setup Steps

### 1. Project Initialization  
- Use Reown’s CLI to bootstrap your AppKit integration:  
```bash
npx @reown/appkit-cli
```
- Select your project name, framework (React or Next.js), and network library (`wagmi` recommended for EVM).  
- Enter project folder and install dependencies:  
```bash
cd your-project-name
npm install
```

### 2. Register Your Project on Reown Cloud  
- Create a free account at [[cloud.reown.com](https://cloud.reown.com/)](https://cloud.reown.com)  
- Create a new AppKit project; obtain the **Project ID** for configuration  

### 3. Environment Configuration  
- Create a `.env` file at the root of your repo, add:  
```env
NEXT_PUBLIC_PROJECT_ID=your-reown-project-id
```

### 4. AppKit Setup in Code  
- Create a config file (e.g., `config/index.tsx`) to set up AppKit with WagmiAdapter and chains:  
```tsx
'use client';

import { createAppKit } from '@reown/appkit/react';
import { WagmiAdapter } from '@reown/appkit-adapter-wagmi';
import { mainnet, arbitrum } from '@reown/appkit/networks';

const wagmiAdapter = new WagmiAdapter({
  projectId: process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_PROJECT_ID!,
  networks: [mainnet, arbitrum],
});

export const appKit = createAppKit({
  adapters: [wagmiAdapter],
  networks: [mainnet, arbitrum],
  projectId: process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_PROJECT_ID!,
  metadata: {
    name: 'Your DApp Name',
    description: 'Description of your dApp',
    url: 'https://your-dapp-url.com',
    icons: ['https://your-dapp-url.com/icon.png'],
  },
  features: {
    analytics: true, // optional
  },
});
```

### 5. Wrap Your Application with AppKit Provider  
- In your React root component (e.g., `_app.tsx` for Next.js), wrap your app with AppKit’s provider for context management:  
```tsx
import { AppKitProvider } from '@reown/appkit/react';

function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }) {
  return (
    
      
    
  );
}
export default MyApp;
```

### 6. UI Integration and Wallet Connect Button  
- Use hooks like `useAppKitAccount()` to query wallet connection state and provide connection UI  
- Build wallet connect/logout buttons in your UI, styled with Chakra UI components for consistency  
- Example minimal connect button snippet:  
```tsx
import { useAppKitAccount, useAppKitModal } from '@reown/appkit/react';
import { Button } from '@chakra-ui/react';

function WalletConnectButton() {
  const { isConnected, disconnect } = useAppKitAccount();
  const modal = useAppKitModal();

  if (isConnected) {
    return Disconnect Wallet;
  }
  return  modal.open()}>Connect Wallet;
}
```

### 7. Handling Authentication & Transactions  
- Utilize AppKit hooks for transaction requests and approvals  
- Monitor wallet status and connect errors for UX feedback with UI toast notifications or modals  

### 8. Testing & Deployment  
- Test wallet connections on supported networks (e.g., Ethereum mainnet, Gnosis)  
- Test user flows: connect, disconnect, request signatures, transaction executions  
- Deploy with environment variables securely configured  

## References and Resources  
- [[Reown AppKit Documentation](https://docs.reown.com/appkit)](https://docs.reown.com/appkit)  
- [[Reown GitHub AppKit Repo](https://github.com/reown-com/appkit)](https://github.com/reown-com/appkit)  
- [[Reown CLI for AppKit Setup](https://docs.monad.xyz/guides/reown-guide)](https://docs.monad.xyz/guides/reown-guide)  
- Sample integrations with wallet connection buttons and React hooks  
- Chakra UI for React components and styling  

[1] https://docs.monad.xyz/guides/reown-guide
[2] https://docs.reown.com/appkit/next/core/installation
[3] https://docs.getpara.com/v2/react/guides/reown-appkit
[4] https://blog.toktokhan.dev/counter-example-using-reown-appkit-with-anchor-framework-e4845d08d175
[5] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-kApLK41MrA

[8] https://docs.tenderly.co/virtual-testnets/dapp-ui/reown-app-kit

title: All Branches Supported for Previews
body:
As of now, we have the ability to deploy and access production and preview deployments.                          i.e. `pay.ubq.fi` and `preview-pay.ubq.fi`

`preview-` is any non default branch deployment.

A useful upgrade will be to support any branch as the prefix instead of collapsing all of them into `preview-` prefixes. i.e. branch name `feat/widget` can deploy to `feat-widget-pay.ubq.fi`

This may require creating a separate Deno deploy project. We MUST have some branch deletion detection, and it deletes the Deno project that corresponds to it to reduce pollution, and save on limits.

title: Implement Differential Reward Distribution for Reopened Issues
body:
When an issue is reopened and closed again, the reward system should calculate and distribute only the difference between previously granted rewards and new rewards.

**Requirements**:
1. Track Previous Rewards
   - Store transaction history with amounts per beneficiary
   - Record payment mode (direct/permit) used for each distribution

2. Differential Calculation
   - Compare new reward amounts with previously distributed amounts
   - Calculate positive differences only (additional rewards)
   - Skip beneficiaries with no change in rewards

3. Distribution Logic
   - Only process transactions for beneficiaries with positive reward differences
   - Handle cases where original payment failed but issue was reopened

4. Error Handling
   - Validate transaction history before processing
   - Handle missing or incomplete previous distribution data
   - Log detailed comparison between old and new reward amounts

**Technical Considerations**:
- Must not conflict with wallet insolvency fallback (generates permits if wallet can't afford payouts)
- Extend Supabase schema to track complete distribution history
- Implement comparison logic in `payment-module.ts`
- Update GitHub comment format to show differential amounts

**Example Scenario**:
```typescript
// Original distribution
{
  "user1": { total: 100, paid: true },
  "user2": { total: 50, paid: true }
}

// New calculation
{
  "user1": { total: 150, difference: 50 },
  "user2": { total: 50, difference: 0 },
  "user3": { total: 25, difference: 25 }
}
```

**Success Criteria**:
- Only processes payments for positive differences
- Provides clear audit trail of all distributions
- Handles edge cases: failed payments, partial distributions should never happen with the insolvency fallback.
- Handle what happens if we change the mode (direct, permits only) between distributions

_Originally posted by @ubiquity-os-beta in https://github.com/ubiquity-os-marketplace/text-conversation-rewards/pull/235#issuecomment-2722879064_

title: GitHub Decoupling
body:
As we start market research on repurposing our tech for other use cases, I realized that if we rely on GitHub for this to work, it limits us to servicing developers. 

I propose building an integration with any popular task management software like asana or even Google Sheets possibly (to save you headache, find one with good APIs and docs) 

This will force you to begin decoupling the architecture from the GitHub dependency. Then this plugin will be portable to other project management software. 

It is not clear to me if our kernel supports this, but assuming that it does, then I imagine these changes can be applied on this plugin in less than a week.

I have mixed feelings on Google Sheets because it will take time to figure out how to make the UX make sense. For example you need to figure how to make a "formula" that can render a button or checkbox, that represents the project is closed as complete. 

I suppose as another project we also need to associate one humans identity across several platforms.

title: Migrate tests from Jest to Bun Test
body:
## Task: Migrate Test Suite from Jest to Bun Test

**Goal:** Replace the current Jest test runner setup with Bun's built-in test runner (`bun test`) for improved performance and simplified tooling.

**Context:**
This project currently uses Jest for running tests. Migrating to `bun test` aligns with the project's adoption of Bun for other tasks (runtime, package management) and can potentially offer faster test execution, plus should help us dealing with ESM import issues and required transformers.

**Required Changes:**

1.  **Remove Jest Dependencies:**
    *   Uninstall Jest-related packages (`jest`, `@types/jest`, `ts-jest`, `babel-jest`, `ts-node` etc.)
    *   Remove the `.babelrc` file
2.  **Remove Jest Configuration:**
    *   Delete the `jest.config.ts` file.
3.  **Update `package.json` Scripts:**
    *   Modify the test script(s) to use `bun test` instead of `jest`.
4.  **Refactor Tests:**
    *   Go through all files in the `tests/` directory.
    *   Replace Jest's global functions (`describe`, `it`, `test`, `expect`, `beforeEach`, `afterEach`, etc.) with imports from `"bun:test"`.
        ```typescript
        import { describe, it, expect, beforeEach, afterEach, mock } from "bun:test";
        ```
    *   Adjust test matchers as needed. While many are similar, there might be differences. Refer to the [Bun Test documentation](https://bun.sh/docs/test/writing).
    *   Update mocking logic. Replace `jest.mock`, `jest.fn`, `jest.spyOn` with Bun's mocking capabilities (e.g., `mock`, `spyOn` from `"bun:test"`).
    *   `mswjs` does not work with `bun` for network mocking. Consider looking into https://github.com/DRFR0ST/bun-bagel or mock Octokit altogether.
5.  **Verify:**
    *   Run `bun test` and ensure all tests pass successfully.
6.  **Update CI/CD:**
    *   Modify `jest-testing.yml` that execute tests to use the new `bun test` command.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

*   All Jest dependencies and configuration files are removed.
*   All tests in the `tests/` directory are successfully migrated to use `bun test` APIs.
*   `bun test` runs without errors and all tests pass.
*   CI/CD pipelines are updated to use `bun test`.

title: Automating Call To Action Delivery (Repo XP Report)
body:
We envision that on our upcoming landing page for the XP product, we need to have a call to action that will convince users to pay for the product. 

The idea is to have them input a repository URL and then we can send them an XP report of the last month for their team. Ideally this would be in the form of a link to the dashboard so they can see everything. 

I imagine that we can leverage the tech from https://github.com/0x4007/travel-stipend/ to trigger a run using text-conversation-rewards https://github.com/ubiquity-os-marketplace/text-conversation-rewards/actions/workflows/compute.yml @gentlementlegen please add any necessary details like what branch its on and how to make this all work together. 

### Details on running `text-conversation-rewards` action

- we need to run this securely either through the kernel or calling directly by providing the proper signature
- there should be a way to know if that was previously run on this repo to avoid running it multiple times
- we can ignore private repositories
- at the end of the run, we can send the results via email to the user
- XP is based on priority / rewards combination, we need a way to define what the labels would have been, theoretically

### Safety

We must only allow this to work once per organization. This is so they can only get this free report one time. In theory this can be circumvented if they transfer repos to new orgs but that seems like a lot of headache and unlikely for established orgs to do.

title: Arbitrage bot
body:
In order to maintain [Dollar token](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/0a230f977ad91bbfb01dd28bc1d2ea77faed5237/packages/contracts/src/dollar/core/UbiquityDollarToken.sol) USD peg [LibUbiquityPool](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/0a230f977ad91bbfb01dd28bc1d2ea77faed5237/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibUbiquityPool.sol) provides the following arbitrage mechanic:
1. If Dollar token USD price >1$ (on a secondary market) then arbitragers are incentivised to 
a) Mint 1 Dollar token for 1 USD of collateral in the [LibUbiquityPool](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/0a230f977ad91bbfb01dd28bc1d2ea77faed5237/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibUbiquityPool.sol)
b) Sell 1 Dollar token on a secondary market
2. On the contrary, If Dollar token USD price <1$ (on a secondary market) then arbitragers are incentivised to:
a) Buy cheap Dollar token on a secondary market
b) Redeem Dollar token for collateral worth 1 USD in the [LibUbiquityPool](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/0a230f977ad91bbfb01dd28bc1d2ea77faed5237/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibUbiquityPool.sol)

On early project stage any large swap may affect the Dollar token price greatly and the pool (where the swap occurred) may remain unbalanced for a long time which in turn depegs the Dollar token.

As a part of this issue we should create an arbitrage bot (i.e. `nodejs` app) which could be run on VPS or any suitable PAAS (heroku, cloudflare, etc...) to catch arbitrage opportunities in order to keep the Dollar token USD pegged on early project stage.

What should be done:
1. Create a new `nodejs` app that will catch arbitrage opportunities for the Dollar token
2. Add unit tests for basic functionality
3. Prepare a clear README of how to run the bot

Notes:
- It makes sense to create the arbitrage bot in a separate repository.
- Deployed contract addresses (mainnet): [here](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/0a230f977ad91bbfb01dd28bc1d2ea77faed5237/packages/contracts/broadcast/Deploy001_Diamond_Dollar_Governance.s.sol/1/run-latest.json)

title: Multi Chain Arbitrage
body:
This bot must target the following markets for now:
1. Mainnet https://www.curve.finance/dex/ethereum/pools/factory-stable-ng-164/swap/
2. Gnosis https://www.curve.finance/dex/xdai/pools/factory-stable-ng-29/swap/

The cross chain mechanism arbitrage is more complex because it would move liquidity back to Mainnet. For example, because now we have UUSD trading at $0.97 on Gnosis:

1. DAI -> bridge -> xDAI -> WXDAI
2. buy UUSD at a 3% discount 
3. bridge UUSD to Mainnet
4. Sell UUSD for LUSD
5. Profit 3% in LUSD

The system would have to estimate all the gas fees involved in the bridging and do this profitably. This will allow UUSD to maintain peg across any chain.

title: Allow user id and installation login
body:
Currently, it is not possible to use an installation token to use this endpoint. This is problematic because that means the bot cannot be used to call this endpoint.

For userId, only the user id number can be used. It would be convenient to also accept the user login to avoid having to always query the user account.

## What is to be done

- accept both id (`12345`) and login (`userLogin`) for the `userId` argument
- handle installation token for logged in requests to the endpoint

This issue also help solving https://github.com/ubiquity-os/plugins-wishlist/issues/75

title: Change the endpoint for `4xx` returns
body:
When querying the endpoint, if a user can't be assigned to the issue on a `GET` it will return a 400 error which is not appropriate. It should be changed to a 200 with the response populated properly, saying the user can be assigned with the reason.

In the response, assigned issues should filter out archived repositories as well.

## What should be done
- [ ] change the `4xx` to `2xx` when appropriate
- [ ] filter out archived repositories from assigned issues
- [ ] fix the user id not being properly used when not using an installation token
- [ ] populate assigned tasks even when the user is not able to start instead of throwing

title: Improve Task Assignment Limit Handling
body:
**Problem**  
Contributors are blocked from assigning new tasks due to the current task limit, even when they’ve addressed all open review threads and are waiting on reviewers. This bottleneck discourages contributors and slows down productivity.

**Proposed Solution**  
1. Add logic to check if the assignee is the last commenter on all unresolved review threads.[^01^]  
2. Introduce a 24-hour timeout after the last comment before considering tasks "reviewer-lagged."  
3. Allow contributors to bypass the limit in such cases without affecting their task slots.

**Concerns**  
- Assignees might misuse this by "resolving" threads without addressing the issues.  
- Additional safeguards may be needed to ensure resolution integrity.

**References**  

- Original comment thread: [#100](https://github.com/ubiquity-os-marketplace/command-start-stop/issues/100#issuecomment-2535532258).




[^01^]: ⚠ 50% possible duplicate - [Improve Task Limiter](https://www.github.com/ubiquity-os-marketplace/command-start-stop/issues/103#103)

title: premade configs that are hands-off for partners
body:
## Premade Configurations vs. Default Plugin Manifests

We aim to streamline the experience for pilot partners by providing **premade configurations**. This approach reduces the need for partners to manually configure plugins. In addition, we provide two alternative ways to modify configurations:

1. Utilize [this feature request](https://github.com/ubiquity-os/plugins-wishlist/issues/65).
2. Contact customer support for manual adjustments.

### Default Plugin Behavior

1. When a plugin uses `defaults` that are **not** sourced from a **premade config**, it retrieves its configuration from the plugin's `manifest.json` in its respective repository.
2. These defaults originate from `pluginInputs.ts` and are used across multiple infrastructure components, including:
   - This UI
   - Natural language configuration edits
   - Plugin schema and input validation (kernel-side)
   - Other relevant subsystems

#### Opinionated Defaults

There is a general concern about **opinionated defaults**. Instead of enforcing such defaults, we should **only share premade configs with partners**, eliminating the need for them to define their own configurations.

Reference: [Discussion on opinionated defaults](https://github.com/ubiquity-os-marketplace/command-start-stop/pull/151#discussion_r1991092437)

### Configuration Modes

Currently, there are three configuration modes:

- **Minimal**
- **Full Defaults**
- **Custom**

These modes, up to this point, have not been enforced to be unopinionated. Some configurations are highly opinionated and may not align with the principle of **non-opinionated defaults**.

---

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Establish a Collection of Premade Configurations
- Define a structured set of **premade configs**.
- Ensure each premade config abstracts its functionality at a high level.
- Reduce the cognitive load on partners.

### 2. YAML-Based Template Structure
To further simplify configuration management, I suggest we use a **YAML template** with the following structure:

```yaml
metadata:
  name: "UbiquityOS Standard Config"
  description: "The fully-enabled and battle-tested configuration which we use across all organizations at Ubiquity."
  version: "1.0"

plugins: ...
```

This template includes:
- **Metadata**: Describes the premade configuration.
- **Plugins**: Lists installed plugins with relevant settings.

This abstraction ensures partners **"don’t have to think about it."**

---

## Handling Plugin Expansion

### Default Non-Expanded Behavior
@gentlementlegen [suggests](https://github.com/ubiquity-os/ubiquity-os-plugin-installer/issues/40#issuecomment-2670919061) that plugins should be installed in a **non-expanded state**. This means:

- **Infra fetches only the non-opinionated defaults** when retrieving the plugin’s manifest.
- **If plugin details are expanded**, infra should fetch its specific config **before** relying on the manifest.

### Supporting Only Premade Configs
If we only allow **premade configs** and restrict editing to **already installed plugins**, then:

- Each `premade` configuration will be expanded by default.
- This ensures we avoid relying on **non-opinionated defaults**, which may not align with intended user experiences.

---

## Conclusion

- **Premade configurations** should be the standard approach.
- A **YAML-based template** simplifies configuration abstraction.
- **Non-expanded installations** should default to non-opinionated configurations.
- **Expanded plugin configurations** should fetch predefined configurations before falling back to defaults.

By enforcing these principles, we create a **streamlined, user-friendly**, and **low-maintenance** approach to plugin configuration management.

title: New Proposal Router
body:
Using vector embeddings(?) we should make a simple UI that will allow us to create any new proposal and it will automatically route it to the correct repository. 

I envision something similar to the new issue ui: https://github.com/ubiquity/.github/issues/new

But the key difference is that we will have a dropdown of where to post the issue to across all of our orgs. It will intelligently hide and show what it thinks is relevant as the user starts typing out the specification, live. 

I imagine with sufficient enough unique detail, we can turn that massive list into something as short as a few target repository options. [^01^] 

Given that we already have issue dedupe tech, I think we can achieve this pretty easily. But it isn't clear to me how to leverage that tech for this solution. This same backend will be immensely useful for the Telegram interface. You can, at any time (even in a dm) write something like: 

```
@ubiquityos we need to fix the cash out interface on pay.ubq.fi because its currently not vertically center aligned. 
```

From there we can preprocess it, and automatically file the proposal...then of course, later it can automatically provide the time/priority/price estimates and distribute it to the DevPool network for somebody to start working. 

[^01^]: ⚠ 78% possible duplicate - [Add support for proposals on `work.ubq.fi` ](https://www.github.com/ubiquity/.github/issues/115#115)

title: Callbacks - event handlers and hybrid plugins
body:
I want to improve the experience of setting up event handlers and hybrid plugins.
I imagine something like this

```js
.on("issue_comment.created", "action", handler1)
.on("issue.closed", "worker", handler2)
```

The SDK would handle calling the Action or run it normally as a Worker and in case you're running it locally it would execute Action handlers locally too. 
Sometimes the reason why we need to call it as an Action is because some libraries are not supported by CF Workers so it'd be also good to have an option to run it as an Action even if you're running it locally.
Current hybrid plugins use `ACTION_REF` to know which Action to call but it'd be great if we could somehow get it automatically but I realize that may be not feasible.

At this point I would also consider merging `createActionsPlugin` and `createPlugin` for seamless experience.

I also realized that maybe we don't need kernel credentials to trigger Action from the Worker but only a fine-grained Github token that would only have access to the repo. Because we don't change the payload but only forward it to the Action, we don't need to generate a new signature and we can keep the original.

title: Automated Sprint Planning
body:
UbiquityOS should be able to manage the team and delegate work autonomously, while keeping tabs on activity, priority levels, and capacity.

### The flow of the vision:
1. When new tasks are posted, we should first run matchmaking to see who is the optimal fit to work on the task. [^1]
2. We should check the top candidate's capacity (how many projects are they already assigned to, and what are their time estimates?)[^2]
   - If they are fully saturated for x amount of time i.e. the next two weeks; overflow to the second top candidate's "work queue"
3. We should always seek to saturate "work queues" of the optimal candidates with the highest priority items.
4. We should also plan out everybody's calendars to saturate 6 hours of work per day, week days.
5. When new tasks are posted, the entire teams' "work queues" should be taken into consideration. We should slot in the new task to the best candidate and bump remainders again based on the matchmaking percentage, and priority levels.
6. Automated follow ups should disqualify and then immediately tweak everybody's "work queues" aka reassign to the next top candidate that isn't saturated with higher priority items.

Expose RESTful APIs on all of the related plugins and use those instead of redundant code. However if this is not technically feasible for some reason, then it might be easiest to make this first version by lifting code from other plugins, and letting this run on `issues.new`.

### Assignment Scoring Algorithm

The scoring algorithm to understand the "inertia" (how movable or immovable a task is from somebody's work queue) of a new task needs to take into account the match percentage, the priority level, and how saturated they are. [^01^]                  We will need to discuss this algorithm.

We should always get the highest priority items queued up first for the highest percentage matched candidate.

[^1]: text-vector-embeddings
[^2]: command-start-stop

title: command-plan
body:
I was experimenting with allowing codex to plan sprints (post all the GitHub Issues with correct labels, parent/child issue relationships, and blocking/blocked by relationships) using the below prompt.

It works quite well and I think there is value to build this out as a capability for our manager. 

We can write /plan and it can take the spec and break it down into child issues. 

Although come to think of it, I think we should have another dedicated plugin for estimating time labels, meaning that this prompt should remove any label related operations if we do go with that approach. 


---

## GitHub Issues & Sprints

This project manages work via GitHub issues and sub‑issues. Keep specs concise and avoid redundancy.

### Hard Rules

- Do not create or apply sprint labels. Do not apply pricing labels.
- Do not prefix titles with sprint codes (e.g., `[S1]`). Titles must be plain and descriptive.
- Do not add tasklists to parent issues; sub‑issues in the UI are the source of truth.
- Use real newlines in bodies (never literal `\n`).

### Minimal Spec Format (child issues)

- Title: descriptive (e.g., `Sorting: clickable headers with asc/desc`).
- Labels: apply exactly one time label (e.g., `Time: <2 Hours`) and one priority label (e.g., `Priority: 3 (High)`).
- Body:
  - First paragraph: one or two sentences describing the change.
  - `Acceptance:` section with short bullet criteria.
  - `Files:` section with bullet list of paths (optionally with starting line), e.g., `- src/web/app.ts:1`.

### Sprint Parent Issues

- Keep the body to the sprint spec only. No tasklists. No title prefixes.
- Link children using sub‑issues only (UI shows them automatically).

### Parent↔Child Linking (Sub‑issues)

- Use REST `sub_issues` endpoints (underscore). The child must be referenced by its REST numeric `id`.

List children:

```
gh api /repos/<REPO>/issues/<PARENT_NUMBER>/sub_issues -q .[].number
```

Add child:

```
child_num=16
rest_id=$(gh api /repos/<REPO>/issues/$child_num -q .id)
gh api -X POST -H "Accept: application/vnd.github+json" \
  /repos/<REPO>/issues/<PARENT_NUMBER>/sub_issues \
  -F sub_issue_id=$rest_id
```

Verify parent of a child:

```
gh api /repos/<REPO>/issues/<CHILD_NUMBER> -q .parent_issue_url
```

### Dependencies (optional)

- If needed, use REST `blocked_by` to express dependencies. Avoid tasklists for dependencies.

### Issue Dependencies (blocked_by / blocking)

Preferred: GraphQL API (works reliably on github.com). Use these standalone prompts (copy/paste) — no external files.

- Add “blocked by” using GraphQL `addBlockedBy`:
```
REPO=<OWNER/REPO>; TARGET=<issue number>; BLOCKER=<issue number>
issue_id=$(gh issue view -R "$REPO" $TARGET --json id -q .id)
blocker_id=$(gh issue view -R "$REPO" $BLOCKER --json id -q .id)
gh api graphql -f query="mutation { addBlockedBy(input: { issueId: \"$issue_id\", blockingIssueId: \"$blocker_id\" }) { issue { number title } blockingIssue { number title } } }"
```

- Remove “blocked by” using GraphQL `removeBlockedBy`:
```
REPO=<OWNER/REPO>; TARGET=<issue number>; BLOCKER=<issue number>
issue_id=$(gh issue view -R "$REPO" $TARGET --json id -q .id)
blocker_id=$(gh issue view -R "$REPO" $BLOCKER --json id -q .id)
gh api graphql -f query="mutation { removeBlockedBy(input: { issueId: \"$issue_id\", blockingIssueId: \"$blocker_id\" }) { issue { number title } } }"
```

- Read dependencies via GraphQL:
```
OWNER=<OWNER>; NAME=<REPO_NAME>; N=<issue number>
gh api graphql -f query="query { repository(owner: \"$OWNER\", name: \"$NAME\") { issue(number: $N) { blockedBy(first: 20) { nodes { number title } } blocking(first: 20) { nodes { number title } } } } }"
```

Notes
- REST endpoints for issue dependencies exist in docs, but may return 404 depending on token type/feature rollout. Prefer GraphQL for portability.

### Label Policy

- Do not create custom labels; read existing labels and choose from them.
- Allowed: exactly one time label (`Time: …`) and one priority label (`Priority: …`) per child issue.
- Disallowed: sprint labels, price labels, and ad‑hoc custom labels unless explicitly requested.
- Parents (sprint umbrella issues): do not set any time label to avoid double counting.

### Time Estimate Policy (Aggressive Defaults)

- Docs and small copy: `Time: <15 Minutes` (use LLM-assisted drafting and quick edits).
- Minor UI wiring (single header behavior, simple handler, or small filter): `Time: <1 Hour`.
- Moderate client work (URL state, drill-through, saved views, CSV export): `Time: <2 Hours`.
- Heavier but contained UI work (print stylesheet, lightweight charts, design tokens, a11y/keyboard, simple virtualization): `Time: <4 Hours`.
- Re-evaluate if scope expands; prefer aggressive estimates by default and adjust upward only with justification in the issue body.

title: Automatically set a `Time: ` label
body:
I ran an experiment of automatically generating subtasks for https://github.com/ubiquity/pay.ubq.fi/issues/421

I told Claude to provide a time estimate based on the issue specification and divide it by 15. So a 15-hour task yields 1 hour. 

Then take a look at the existing `Time: ` labels of the repository and label with the best fitting one.

---

Due to some problems with Claude this week (horrible at following instructions, rumored that they are quantizing the model to save on costs this last week) I switched over to Grok. Grok code seemed to not need the time offset. 

---

Make a plug-in that listens to issue created or specification edited and automatically sets a time label based on similar instructions.

---

I do prefer using Claude because it's included with my Claude Code 20x Max plan. 

Try getting it to work with `claude -p "my prompt here`"! I added a couple extra hours here cause of prompting and testing. I imagine if it only has to worry about a single issue, it should be better at following instructions. 


Another remark is that I specifically tried to remove bias by telling it to ignore the original time estimate listed. Just provide a new estimate.

Make the offset configurable, because I imagine that as the models improve, this offset will change.

title: Check dev experience on starting an issue
body:
There are certain kinds of tasks which must be completed only by experienced developers.

Check the following issues for example:
- https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/issues/927
- https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/issues/563

It would be great to know that collaborator solving the above issues has prior experience with solidity and ethereum.

Possible solution could be to use chatgpt to parse collaborator's github or CV to make sure a contributor is experienced enough to `/start` an issue.

title: Config Protection
body:
Not sure if this is fully possible from within the webhook event, but the idea is that only admins or billing managers should be able to modify the config because it affects money flow. 

On commit, check if config was modified. If it is unauthorized, rollback the change by immediately committing the previous version as the UbiquiBot. 

This should make it near impossible for fraud. 

---

Seems like [relevant logic](https://github.com/ubiquibot/assistive-pricing/blob/d259e29b2896026a164e7c1af4f2b72ce31fe90c/src/handlers/get-base-rate-changes.ts) I saw in [a pull](https://github.com/ubiquibot/assistive-pricing/pull/18/files#).

title: Generalized "GitHub Webhook + Contributor Role -> Rewards" No Config v1
body:
Dynamically map the config property name to count the amount of matching webhook events that occur in the issue/pull timeline, and credit accordingly. In which case, this plugin seems generally useful for mapping any value to any event, in the context of an issue or pull! 

All this is intended to do is:
1. get the timeline of events from the issue and the linked pulls
2. assign a value of `1` to every event that is counted, per contributor.
3. return sum totals. 

In the next iteration, we should identify the user's "class" (specification author, assignee, collaborator, contributor)

In the final iteration, we should enable config. 

- All [webhook events](https://github.com/octokit/webhooks.js/blob/main/src/generated/webhook-names.ts) reference.

title: Generalized "GitHub Webhook + Contributor Role -> Rewards" Contributor Class v2
body:
Extending on https://github.com/ubiquity-os/plugins-wishlist/issues/46 we should be able to identify the user's "class"

> In the next iteration, we should identify the user's "class"

1. `specification author` - the original author of the task
2. `assignee` - they are the one responsible for the deliverable
3. `collaborator` - they are added to the org/repo as an official team member
4. `contributor` - the default option

title: Generalized "GitHub Webhook + Contributor Role -> Rewards" Unit Tests
body: The final stop is to write comprehensive unit tests for everything. Small tests are expected in the other deliverables, but this is intended to cover any odds and ends leftover.

title: Pull/Task Matching
body:
If there's unassigned tasks (priced issues) and a pull is opened against a specific repo, I imagine that by looking at the diff we might be able to reasonably guess what task the pull is associated with. 

This will require some research to get the accuracy high enough for this to be useful but I'm fairly optimistic because it will not have so many task specifications to guess against.

Once this is quite accurate, then it will be a very seamless experience for contributors to just focus on opening pulls and UbiquityOS handles the rest including all the necessary payouts etc. 

I think that the configuration can have some type of confidence threshold I.e. 50% before it will try and associate. This will prevent newly opened pulls with barely any changes to try and match unrelated results. 

After some more commits, it can start adding a comment under the pull (not sure if this requires the contributor to have installed UbiquityOS on their repo?) explaining that the pull is likely to be associated with X issue (this also should only run if it is not already manually linked to an issue)

title: Handling old proposals
body:
Very rarely I'll go through https://devpool.directory/?proposal=true to review old proposals and see if they are still relevant. 

In the past I've asked the team to handle this but in practice this never happens. 

We should have some mechanism similar to disqualifier that 1. Asks for an update and 2. Closes it if no response.

title: Security monitoring
body:
In case of a security incident we should:
1. Pause all of the contracts
2. Notify the core team that something went wrong

Possible solutions for monitoring:
- https://docs.chain.link/chainlink-automation
- https://www.openzeppelin.com/defender
- https://cyvers.ai/

What should be done:
1. Setup monitoring for [LibUbiquityPool](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/a1b11e1b944227144d7cc19ab320220d9fce8b69/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibUbiquityPool.sol). [^01^]                                                                                                                                            In case of a possible security incident (let's say >30% of liquidity is withdrawn) we should:
a) Pause the [UbiquityDollarToken](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/a1b11e1b944227144d7cc19ab320220d9fce8b69/packages/contracts/src/dollar/core/UbiquityDollarToken.sol)
b) Pause [LibUbiquityPool](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/a1b11e1b944227144d7cc19ab320220d9fce8b69/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibUbiquityPool.sol) by [disabling collateral](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/a1b11e1b944227144d7cc19ab320220d9fce8b69/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibUbiquityPool.sol#L1129)
2. Send notification to https://t.me/UbiquityDAO (you may create a new topic there like `Dollar monitoring`)

P.S. Mainnet contract addresses can be found [here](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/e4c1fae5ef5e9ba40bcb84c671f2ba39f0ab5208/packages/contracts/broadcast/Deploy001_Diamond_Dollar_Governance.s.sol/1/run-latest.json)

title: Integrate Liquity V1 Stability Pool for LUSD Collateral Yield
body:
## Problem
Plain LUSD collateral earns 0% yield. Integrate Stability Pool for ~6.28% APR ([Dune data](https://dune.com/liquity/liquity-v1)) to fund governance token buybacks, without disrupting liquidity.

## Solution
Add `StabilityPoolFacet` via diamond proxy. Auto-deposit LUSD to pool on mint; withdraw principal on redeem. Harvest ETH/LQTY yields during redeems for buybacks/compounding (protocol-owned only). Piggyback ops on user txs for gas efficiency.

## Alternatives Considered
- AAVE (~3.24% yield, more complex).
- Leverage/LPs (higher risk).
- V2 migration (future option for multi-collateral).

## Implementation Spec
### ~~Prerequisites~~
- ~~Audit facet (~$10-20K budget).~~
- ~~Integrate `IStabilityPool.sol` (address: 0x66017D22b0f8556afDd19e1e5b5f1cbD89a6C337).~~
- ~~Add 1inch/Uniswap for swaps.~~
- ~~New storage: `totalPrincipalInPool` (uint256), `protocolTreasury` (address).~~

### Facet Functions
- `depositToPool(uint256 amount)`: Call `provideToSP(amount, address(0))`.
- `withdrawFromPool(uint256 amount)`: Call `withdrawFromSP(amount)`.
- `harvestRewards()`: Claim rewards, swap (e.g., 50% to LUSD for compounding, 50% to governance token for buybacks).

### Flows
- **Mint**: Transfer LUSD → Deposit to pool → Mint uUSD → Update `totalPrincipalInPool += amount`.
- **Redeem**: Burn uUSD → Calc principal → Withdraw/transfer principal → Harvest if rewards > threshold (route to treasury) → Update `totalPrincipalInPool -= amount`.

### Over-Collateralization
Value pool as LUSD equivalent (Chainlink oracles for ETH/LQTY). Redemptions: principal only.

### Monitoring/Automation
- Chainlink/Gelato for optional harvests. It would be nice to sell the ETH/LQTY for LUSD and boost holdings as soon as it is rewarded by the stability pool.
- Dune alerts for APR/supply.

## Testing
- Unit: Deposit/withdraw/harvest (Foundry).
- Integration: Mainnet fork; simulate liquidations.
- Edges: Low liquidity, volatility, mass redeems. Gas: <200K extra per tx.

## Deployment
1. Deploy facet.
2. `diamondCut` via multisig.
3. Verify on Etherscan.
4. Phased: Opt-in for new deposits.

## ~~Risks/Mitigations~~
- ~~Yield variability: Fallback to plain LUSD if APR <3%.~~
- ~~Supply crunch: Auto-withdraw if < $10M.~~
- ~~Security: Audit for reentrancy.~~

title: Staking v2 production deploy
body:
Depends on https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/issues/994

We should deploy staking feature to production.

What to do:
1. Make sure the latest https://github.com/foundry-rs/foundry is installed
2. Make sure you're on the latest `development` branch of https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar
3. Make sure the `packages/contracts/.env` file contains the `OWNER_PRIVATE_KEY` env variable (which is the private key for the `ubq.eth` address which has both the `owner` and `admin` roles):
```
# Owner private key (grants access to updating Diamond facets and setting TWAP oracle address).
OWNER_PRIVATE_KEY="OWNER_PRIVATE_KEY"
```
4. From the `packages/contracts` folder run:
```
forge script migrations/mainnet/Deploy002_Staking.s.sol:Deploy002_Staking --rpc-url https://mainnet.gateway.tenderly.co --broadcast -vvvv
```
5. Commit broadcast logs

It seems this task is for @0x4007

title: Governance Token emissions to `ubq.eth` new strategy
body:
For every `1` governance token minted via our staking contract to our community members, we emit an additional `0.5` governance tokens to `ubq.eth`

I think it could be a very interesting proof of concept for future DAOs to fund development automatically by emitting governance tokens to a wallet that is controlled by the UbiquiBot for their repositories. 

What would be the simplest way to add an additional split, but within the protocol level? 

To clarify, this is a low priority task that would be used primarily for selling a future vision (this is why I think it should be implemented on the smart contract level.)

It would be great if we can set multiple emission destinations and set the amounts. That way, for example, maintenance on this repository can receive 5% of all governance token emissions, UbiquiBot repository 10% etc. 

### Context

- Here is a recent [reward claim](https://etherscan.io/tx/0xb172ee408a0129d635b2ffd6f4ffd673573761956d6ae099a18ed1e22843554b).
- https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/7a70182d49a0b9dc947c9925a5d28edc49c28b0d/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibChef.sol#L369-L372

title: Formal verification
body:
We should implement formal verification for [LibUbiquityPool](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/a1b11e1b944227144d7cc19ab320220d9fce8b69/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibUbiquityPool.sol). 

So collaborator who's going to take this issue should carefully study the [LibUbiquityPool](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/a1b11e1b944227144d7cc19ab320220d9fce8b69/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibUbiquityPool.sol) and think of the invariants that should be tested.

Invariants could be:
- user can not mint more Dollars in USD than provided collateral
- user can not redeem more collateral in USD than provided Dollar tokens

Possible solutions for formal verification:
- https://github.com/a16z/halmos
- https://docs.runtimeverification.com/kontrol

We already had a try with formal verification, you may find related scripts [here](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/tree/a1b11e1b944227144d7cc19ab320220d9fce8b69/packages/contracts/scripts/smt-checker).

What should be done:
1. Impelent formal verification for the [LibUbiquityPool](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/a1b11e1b944227144d7cc19ab320220d9fce8b69/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibUbiquityPool.sol) contract (you may create tests in a separate test file like `UbiquityPoolFacet.formal.t.sol` so we could distinguish unit, fuzz, invariant and formal verification tests)
2. Setup testing CI to run formal verification tests on merge to the `development` branch (keep in mind that github action runnners can run for 6 hours). We don't need to run formal verification tests on eash PR since they're really time consuming.

title: Final Pre-Seed/Seed Investor Debt UBQ
body:
> So what's next steps to watch out for? Do we need to fix the amounts again when the stakes are withdrawn?

Finally!

The next 'final' batch should be done after bonds expiry. Only then the remaining exact payout amounts will be available.

The algorithm will be:

1) Update network block number in https://github.com/gitcoindev/uad-contracts/blob/staking-mutliplier-fix/tasks/simulateBondingDebt.ts and execute the script again using the following command:

`$ npx hardhat simulateBondingDebt`

This will fork the blockchain, get the current inflation rate and output missing stake values for the same set of bonds. What was paid / disbursed today, should be subtracted from the amount that will be shown.

2) The BondingDebtV2 / BondingDebtFinal contract will be deployed in the same way BondingDebt, but with remaining UBQ values for the bond holders.

title: CI: fix `check_storage_layout` for new contracts
body:
There's the [core-contracts-storage-check](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/20799bedf5ee4d40d2863653f7bc2fe68a2ce3fa/.github/workflows/core-contracts-storage-check.yml) workflow which checks that contracts' storage is not messed on contract upgrades.

Check these CI runs which fails with the `No workflow run found with an artifact named...` error:
- https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/actions/runs/11125075713/job/30912062971?pr=959
- https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/actions/runs/11145695030/job/30975996318?pr=971

The issue is that when a **new contract** is added the [core-contracts-storage-check](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/20799bedf5ee4d40d2863653f7bc2fe68a2ce3fa/.github/workflows/core-contracts-storage-check.yml) starts to fail while the expected behavior for the CI workflow to pass because we don't need to check storage layout for newly added contracts (because storage of the newly added contract hasn't been updated hence storage collision could not occur).

So as a part of this issue we should make sure that:
1. [core-contracts-storage-check](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/20799bedf5ee4d40d2863653f7bc2fe68a2ce3fa/.github/workflows/core-contracts-storage-check.yml) is not failing for newly added contracts
2. [diamond-storage-check.yml](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/20799bedf5ee4d40d2863653f7bc2fe68a2ce3fa/.github/workflows/diamond-storage-check.yml) is not failing for newly added libraries

P.S. Pls provide a QA in the related PR with these scenarios:
1) No storage updates, CI passing
2) Storage update, no collision, CI passing
3) Storage update, collision, CI failing
4) New contract added, CI passing

title: Add `UUSD` and `UBQ` tokens to popular services
body:
Right now when you interact with DEXes or web3 wallets you have to paste UUSD/UBQ addresses manually in order to interact with those tokens. 

We should make sure that [UUSD](https://etherscan.io/address/0xb6919Ef2ee4aFC163BC954C5678e2BB570c2D103) and [UBQ](https://etherscan.io/address/0x4e38D89362f7e5db0096CE44ebD021c3962aA9a0) tokens are displayed "natively" in popular services in order to put less friction on our users.

We should support:
- DEXes:
  -  CoW Swap ([UUSD request](https://github.com/cowprotocol/token-lists/issues/797), [UBQ request](https://github.com/cowprotocol/token-lists/issues/798))
  -  Uniswap (mainnet: added [UUSD/DAI](https://app.uniswap.org/explore/pools/ethereum/0xf6cff717edaba14b47b8159066d947d6ef3ab7d1825c13d8294c1e544ddeacd6) and [UBQ/DAI](https://app.uniswap.org/explore/pools/ethereum/0x41193ad7ace7cc2f8cf262afc5da61f999a77350914604d22cb006251cd20c76) pools)
  -  Curve (done)
  -  Balancer (gnosis: added [UUSD/WXDAI](https://balancer.fi/pools/gnosis/v3/0x1545815a9Df066bD58d5d47EfF7ADe0E2A9A6Bbb) and [UBQ/WXDAI](https://balancer.fi/pools/gnosis/v3/0x86CACC8d8c1f84D71Ca1dc1Bd9B65b3eCe57Be79) pools)
  -  SushiSwap (gnosis: added [UUSD/XDAI](https://www.sushi.com/gnosis/pool/v3/0x39c3dEe55E7cFB678E53402b9596ACdb3f3bAa05/1046) and [UBQ/XDAI](https://www.sushi.com/gnosis/pool/v3/0x5c045392502c0b371f52386e8cE31829218bDf14/1047) pools)
  -  Bancor (only mainnet)
  -  PancakeSwap (only mainnet)
  -  1inch (contacted [support](https://help.1inch.io/en/articles/4878336-how-to-get-a-token-listed-on-1inch))
- web3 wallets: 
  - metamask ([UUSD request](https://github.com/MetaMask/contract-metadata/pull/1413), [UBQ request](https://github.com/MetaMask/contract-metadata/pull/1414))
  - coinbase (requires https://www.coingecko.com or https://coinmarketcap.com listing)
  - trustwallet (token list from https://github.com/trustwallet/assets, 580$ listing fee per token)
- bridges: 
  - https://bridge.gnosischain.com (contacted support)
  - https://app.debridge.finance (contacted support)
  - https://jumper.exchange (contacted support)
  - https://app.shapeshift.com (token list is taken from https://www.coingecko.com)
  - https://www.bungee.exchange (token list is taken from https://www.coingecko.com)
  - https://li.fi (done)
- price charts:
  - https://www.coingecko.com ([rejected](https://gist.github.com/rndquu/75289f88a63bc557fa3fc6a555f4fa8d), try again after 6 Feb)
  - https://coinmarketcap.com ([takes](https://coinmarketcap.com/dexscan) data on-chain from Uniswap, sent an inquiry but they responded with an invoice for $5k)
  - https://dexscreener.com (token list is taken from https://www.coingecko.com/)

Most of those services take token info from centralized github repositories (sometimes it's a paid service). We should put UUSD and UBQ tokens info into those repositories which serve as sources for DEXes, wallets, etc...

title: Import Nonces
body:
I was working on [this](https://github.com/ubiquity/pay.ubq.fi/issues/413#issuecomment-3240339002) and I realized that a redeployment could be nice to have to: 

1. rename the smart contract to permit3 for disambiguation.
2. Allow us to import already used Nonces as an array (intended to clone the used ones on mainnet and gnosis chain) during deployment, 
3. Add a method we can call later for batch nonce imports. This beats having to send thousands of individual invalidation requests.  
4. A versioning setter, so we can leave a comment if its deprecated etc.

I hope its easy to batch read the entire nonce bitmap in a single RPC call, otherwise this can be tedious.

title: Bundle Performance Split – Handoff
body:
# Bundle Performance Split – Handoff

Objective
- Reduce the main JS bundle size via code‑splitting and vendor chunking while preserving current functionality.

Context
- Build produced a large main chunk (~543 kB minified; see build output).
- Heavy deps likely include wallet/connectors and query libs.

Deliverables
- Vite/Rollup configuration to split vendor chunks (e.g., `manualChunks`) for wallet/connectors and other heavy libs.
- Lazy load connector UI and other rarely used routes/components.
- A short report in this file (append at bottom) with the before/after sizes from `bun run build`.

Acceptance Criteria
- Main application chunk (non-vendor) gzip size reduced meaningfully (target < 140 kB gz if feasible; any significant reduction acceptable if functionality unchanged).
- App still builds and runs (`bun run dev` and `bun run build` both OK).
- No broken dynamic imports.

Constraints & Guidance
- Do not regress DX: dev server should remain fast.
- Be careful with CSS splitting to avoid FOUC; keep existing styles intact.
- Avoid over‑engineering; start by lifting large deps into separate vendor chunks.

Suggested Steps
1) Inspect `node_modules` imports via `vite-bundle-visualizer` or similar (optional).
2) Update `vite.config.ts` → `build.rollupOptions.output.manualChunks` to separate:
   - `react` + `react-dom`
   - `@tanstack/react-query`
   - `wagmi` + `viem` + connectors (walletconnect/coinbase)
3) Convert wallet UI to lazy import where appropriate.
4) Rebuild and record sizes in this document.

Validation
- Commands:
  - `bun run build`
  - Confirm chunk list and sizes in terminal output.

Report (fill in)
- Before: main chunk gzip size ≈ 168 kB (from prior build).
- After: main chunk gzip size ≈ ___ kB.
- Notes: ____.

title: Error Handling & Status Toasts – Handoff
body:
# Error Handling & Status Toasts – Handoff

Objective
- Implement consistent error/success handling and user feedback via lightweight toasts/snackbars across key flows (RPC read failures, approve/stake actions, reward claim), without introducing large UI kits.

Context
- Repo: `stake.ubq.fi` (branch `pr-1`)
- Existing context provider: `src/context/status-message.tsx` (extend or replace with a small toast system)
- Styles live under `src/css/*`; maintain visual consistency.

Deliverables
- A minimal toast system (provider + hook) with variants: `info`, `success`, `error`.
- Wrap top‑level app with the provider; expose `useToast()` for components.
- Standardize error surfaces:
  - Network/RPC errors (query layer) → show succinct error with retry guidance.
  - Transaction flows (approve/stake/claim) → show pending → success/failure.
- Ensure messages collapse duplicates and auto‑dismiss after 4–6s (except persistent critical errors).

Acceptance Criteria
- Clear, non‑noisy toasts appear for simulated failures (e.g., forced RPC error) and success flows.
- No console spam or unhandled promise rejections.
- Visual style aligns with existing CSS; no heavyweight UI library added.

Constraints & Guidance
- Reuse TanStack Query error boundaries/`onError` handlers where possible.
- Avoid large dependencies; implement ~100–200 lines max for the core toast system.
- Keep i18n/out‑of‑scope polish minimal; focus on correctness and UX clarity.

Suggested Steps
1) Create `src/ui/toast.tsx` (provider, portal, hook) and `src/css/toast.css`.
2) Wrap `<App />` with provider in `src/main.tsx`.
3) Wire calls in strategic places (pool actions, fetches) and consolidate error shapes.
4) Add a few unit tests around the toast hook with Bun test.

Validation
- Dev: `bun run dev`, manually trigger success/error; verify toasts.
- Unit: `bun test` for hook behavior and dedup logic.

Out of Scope
- Accessibility/a11y polish beyond basic focus and readability.

title: E2E Smoke (Playwright) – Handoff
body:
# E2E Smoke (Playwright) – Handoff

Objective
- Add a minimal Playwright E2E suite that validates the app loads, renders the home view, and shows a WalletConnect/Reown connect‑wallet entry point in both `dev` and preview modes.

Context
- Repo: `stake.ubq.fi` (branch `pr-1`)
- Dev: `bun run dev` (http://localhost:5173)
- Preview: `bun run build` then serve `dist/` via `vite preview`

Deliverables
- Playwright installed and configured for Chromium only (keep it small to start).
- Two tests:
  - `loads-homepage`: Page title contains “Stake” or app brand and root content renders.
  - `connect-wallet-visible`: A visible WalletConnect/Reown connect trigger (e.g., a button named “Connect”) is present; do not require an actual wallet connection.
- Scripts in `package.json`:
  - `e2e`: `playwright test`
  - `e2e:ui`: `playwright test --ui`
  - `preview`: `vite preview --port 4173`
- Helper to auto-start server:
  - For dev: launch `bun run dev` before tests, wait for `http://localhost:5173`.
  - For preview: `bun run build` then `bunx vite preview` before tests, wait for `http://localhost:4173`.

Acceptance Criteria
- `bunx playwright test` passes locally against preview (build + preview).
- Tests run headless in CI and skip if port unavailable (clear error message).
- No dependence on wallets, RPC keys, or network calls.

Constraints & Guidance
- Prefer using a test fixture that starts/stops the server.
- Use a generous timeout on server startup (e.g., 20–30s).
- Avoid brittle selectors; match on role/name for the connect button (WalletConnect/Reown UI). Do not include Coinbase‑specific selectors.

Suggested Steps
1) Add devDeps: `@playwright/test`.
2) Generate minimal config `playwright.config.ts` with baseURL switching via env (`E2E_MODE=dev|preview`).
3) Create `tests/e2e/*.spec.ts` with two smoke tests.
4) Implement lightweight server launcher in `tests/e2e/fixtures/server.ts` (child_process + wait-on).
5) Add scripts and document how to run both modes in repo README or a new `docs/testing.md`.

Validation
- Commands:
  - Dev: `E2E_MODE=dev bunx playwright test`
  - Preview: `E2E_MODE=preview bunx playwright test`

Out of Scope
- Wallet interactions or chain forking/anvil; that belongs to a separate task.

# Unit Tests (Bun Test) – Handoff

Objective
- Establish a minimal, fast unit test baseline using Bun’s built‑in test runner.

Context
- Repo: `stake.ubq.fi` (branch `pr-1`)

- Ensure a DOM‑like environment (Bun provides a browser‑compatible runtime); if jsdom is preferred, configure via preload/setup.

Suggested Steps

2) Create `test/setup.ts` to register `@testing-library/jest-dom` and set up any required DOM shims.

title: RPC Robustness & Fallback – Handoff
body:
# RPC Robustness & Fallback – Handoff

Objective
- Harden RPC configuration and runtime behavior: validate env resolution, add fallbacks, timeouts, and basic health checks to reduce flaky UX.

Context
- Repo: `stake.ubq.fi` (branch `pr-1`)
- Current rules documented in README and implemented in `src/constants/config.ts`.
- Modes: `dev` (public RPC), `local-node` (http://localhost:8545, chain 31337), `prod` (relative `/rpc`).

Deliverables
- Strengthen `src/constants/config.ts`:
  - Validate `VITE_RPC_URL` format; surface clear console warnings when misconfigured.
  - Implement a small fallback list for mainnet (e.g., `https://rpc.ubq.fi`) when the primary URL fails a quick HEAD/eth_chainId query.
  - Respect `local-node` mode with no chain suffixing.
- Add a lightweight `rpcHealthCheck()` utility to test `eth_chainId` with a short timeout (e.g., 1–2s) and return typed status.
- Optional: a diagnostic panel in dev builds to display detected RPC, chainId, and recent error counts (hidden in prod).

Acceptance Criteria
- In dev mode with an invalid `VITE_RPC_URL`, app logs a clear warning and falls back to a known-good endpoint (if allowed by the mode rules).
- In local-node mode, health check targets `http://localhost:8545` and indicates unavailable state if anvil is not running, without crashing the app.
- No breaking changes to existing consumers; exported config remains backward compatible.

Constraints & Guidance
- Avoid introducing heavy deps; use native fetch with abort/timeout.
- Keep logic well‑typed and small; unit test the key branches with Bun test.

Suggested Steps
1) Update `src/constants/config.ts` with validation and fallback logic.
2) Create `src/utils/rpc-health.ts` with `rpcHealthCheck()` using `eth_chainId`.
3) Add unit tests for `dev`, `local-node`, and `prod` behaviors.
4) Optionally add a small dev‑only panel in `src/components/dev-rpc-panel.tsx`.

Validation
- Unit: `bun test` passes for config and health utilities.
- Dev: `bun run dev` with broken URL shows graceful fallback or readable error.
